entry.title: SAP at Hackfestival 2024 - Technology & Sustainability Coming Together to Find Innovative Solutions
entry.content:
The Hackfestival 2024 is a highly anticipated tech event that took place in Mannheim between September 6-8. Under the motto “Decarbonize the Planet”, the festival aimed to bring together tech enthusiasts, sustainability experts, coders, and hackers to collaborate and innovate in sustainability related hands-on challenges during a full-weekend hackathon.  

Located at Congress Center Rosengarten, it combined a competitive hackathon and free citizen track with a wide range of engaging activities for the greater public - including an exhibition area, workshops, open discussions and seminars. Participants had the opportunity to network with other tech talents and industry leaders, share their ideas, grow their career, test their skills and learn from each other. 

Apart from that, the event was also a great opportunity to bring citizens closer to sustainability in general as well as individual corporate initiatives. As an event partner, we had many valuable exchanges around SAP’s own journey to net-zero and the SAP Sustainability product portfolio at our booth. It is always meaningful to see the interest in this topic, make a contribution and provide deeper insights into corporate sustainability and thus make it more tangible for a larger audience. 

SAP team at our SAP Sustainability boothSome key facts and figures around the Hackfestival: 

  • Organized by The Hackathon Company – an independent European hackathon-as-a-service provider  
  • 300+ participants 
  • 35+ partners (Google, aws, Roche and more) 
  • 48-hours of hacking 
  • Six sustainability challenges 
  • Price pool of 10.000+ Euros 

The event was kicked-off on Friday with an opening ceremony and inspiring speech by Alexander Gerst, who talked about his journey as an ESA astronaut followed by Christian Specht, mayor of the city of Mannheim. Gunther Rothermel, co-General Manager and Chief Product Officer for SAP Sustainability, and other industry channel partners were part of a fruitful panel discussion, giving insights about the corporate view on sustainability and how technology and innovation can help take climate action and make a difference in the world.  

Gunther said: “With increasing regulatory landscapes and reporting requirements, our customers have set themselves a broad set of challenging sustainability targets...and we did the same at SAP. There is a lot we can address with software and smart approaches to embed sustainability into digital business processes...First we wanted to start by acting as an exemplar, working on our own emissions and journey to net-zero as a company...in the second step, we help our customers to become more sustainable and accompany them with our SAP Sustainability products. Imagine that SAP customers generate 87% of total global commerce....By integrating sustainability more naturally into customers transactions, we can have a huge impact on decarbonization, reduction of waste and more together. So, both these sides matter to us.”  

The panel talk has clearly shown - at SAP, we incorporate sustainability in our strategy as well as our core values and are proud to have a strong internal SAP Community that engages in voluntary projects and lives sustainability in its daily actions, because every contribution, no matter how small, can change things. 

According to this discussion, the focus of our sustainability effort is based on four key areas:  

  • Taking Climate Action and Reducing Carbon Emissions 
  • Establishing a Circular Economy to reuse material and avoid waste 
  • Integrating a Social Responsibility dimension to drive socially responsible value chains, promote respect for human rights, and create positive societal impacts.
  • Holistic Steering & Reporting: Acting on the operational layer to be compliant and safe on the production and plant level as well as in company's own transactions and processes to be transparent on the way to net-zero. 

Sustainability @SAP

Afterwards the different challenges were introduced and kicked off by the companies. The highly motivated participants had 48 hours to solve challenges provided by the “Challenge Setters” under the main topic “Decarbonize the Planet”. Divided into teams they started working on sustainability solutions, including design and business perspectives.  

Coding has started

SAP' challenge: „𝐇𝐞𝐲, 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞’𝐬 𝐜𝐚𝐫𝐛𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐦𝐲 𝐥𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐡🍴”: 

We provided the following task: “As one-third of global emissions stem from the food system, imagine the impact that can be made by addressing this challenge head-on – especially when there’s the catering needs of over 110,000 employees. ​The challenge for the participants is to develop a digital solution that helps decarbonize the planet by reducing emissions within the food system or specifically targeting food waste.” 

The SAP Challenge

Heads were put together, many innovative ideas were developed and there were no limits to creativity. In the end, we received a lot of valuable ideas, and the following team was among the winners:  

SAP challenge team: "FrostGuardians": Food waste comes from the fact that everyone wastes food every day, so with the help of AI and a camera, the team developed a fridge that manages what's in the fridge, gives tips on food that goes with a recipe and is connected to a shopping list. 

SAP team and the winnersBig congratulations to the team and all other winners for the inspiring and creative solutions. 🏅

"FrostGuardians" the proud winners of the SAP challenge

One of the main goals of the festival was to foster creativity and problem-solving skills among participants and to encourage people to develop solutions with a positive social impact. Many attendees left the festival feeling inspired and motivated to continue their work in the tech industry. 

The Hackfestival 2024 in Mannheim was a fantastic opportunity for tech enthusiasts to come together and push the boundaries of innovation. It provided a platform where like-minded individuals could collaborate and drive positive change. 

It was a pleasure to support this event as a partner. A hackathon is a great opportunity to access collective knowledge, promote creativity and team spirit, resulting in digital applications and innovative ideas that sometimes can even be the start of something big. 

There is only one thing left to say - A big thank you goes to the organizational team and all participants. Only together we can make a difference. 🌍 🌱

Please see the full list of all other winners' solutions (shared by Oliver Bruemmer, Founder & CEO The Hackathon Company):
 
Trash Buddy by Stadt Mannheim/ Metropolregion Rhein-Neckar: Scan products you want to throw away and get tips on what you can do with them. Find other solutions and don’t only throw it away. 
 
Mannheim Green City Quest by Stadt Mannheim/ Metropolregion Rhein-Neckar: Playful knowledge transfer app that encourages young people to try out new ways of thinking and exploring the city in a sustainable way with geolocations and gamification elements. 
 
Rochy - The Carbon Coach by Roche : "Rochy" gives sustainability reminders and awards points when fulfilled and proven with pictures, e.g. plate eaten empty. Collected points lead to coupons or can be donated.  
Carbon Crasher by Schwarz IT KG: Machine learning app that recommends products based on your interests, such as products that save CO2 or have a short expiration date, with special prices, coupons and gamification. 
 
KAEFER by KION Group: Efficient LLM model with key features: dynamic scoring of models, prompt model complexity matching, ML-based prompt categorization, self-learning architecture interaction.

Learn more about SAP Sustainability and subscribe to weekly Sustainability Highlight Newsletter.

entry.title: SAP Build Extensibility Wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Target Group

Anyone interested to know more about extending SAP S/4HANA Cloud using SAP Build

 

Background

What is Extensibility

When a customer has unique business requirements that are not fully catered by standard SAP S/4HANA then there is a need to extend the system. These extensions can be classified into 2 broad categories:

  • On-stack extensibility involves extending SAP S/4HANA directly within the core system using key user tools or ABAP.
  • Side-by-side extensibility uses SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to develop custom applications or integrations outside the core S/4HANA system, ensuring greater flexibility and upgrade safety.

What is SAP Build

SAP Build ( including SAP Build Code) is the default extension development toolset on SAP BTP.

Build Tools under low-code spectrum:

  • SAP Build Apps - Create Mobile and Web applications
  • SAP Build Process Automation - Automate business processes and workflows
  • SAP Build Work Zone - Create Digital Workspaces and business sites

Build Tools under Pro-code spectrum:

SAP Build Code - Use Business Application Studio(BAS) and Cloud Application programming Model(CAP) to create Applications. And lot more.

What challenges are we trying to address here

  • Not easy to identify the available extension options for a given S/4HANA application directly in the system
  • Not easy to determine the right extension points (eg APIs, Events) for a given S/4HANA application
  • Not an integrated experience when extending SAP S/4HANA in a side-by-side manner on BTP

 

Introduction

  • A new Extensibility wizard is available with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408 that provides both on-stack and side-by-side extension options consolidated into a single wizard.
  • It provides a guided experience to kick start extension creation and enables seamless navigation from SAP S/4HANA to SAP Build.
  • The wizard understands which S/4HANA application it has been started from and accordingly filters the extensions points( like API, Events) based on the business context.

This wizard can be accessed from any extensible SAP S/4HANA app as shown below. "Create Extension" option is available under the Me Area (User menu) on the top right.

On clicking the Create Extension option, the Extensibility Wizard opens up displaying the various extension options

Note: Setup steps mentioned next are needed as a prerequisite to be able to see the "Create Extension" option

 

Setup

Short summary of the setup steps:

  1. Subscribe to SAP Build Process Automation in your SAP BTP subaccount.
  2. Add your SAP S/4HANA cloud system in the BTP Global account under System landscape and register in a formation.
  3. Create 2 destinations in the BTP account pointing to UI service and API service of SAP S/4HANA. This is needed for communication from Build to SAP S/4HANA, for example to fetch business context, events and authorizations.
  4. Create a communication arrangement in SAP S/4HANA. This is to enable communication from SAP S/4HANA to SAP Build.
  5. Assign the required authorization in SAP S/4HANA to access the wizard.

Detailed setup steps:

  • BTP setup steps - link
  • S4 setup steps - link

 

Extension options in the Wizard ( Sep 2024 )

On-stack

Custom Field

Enables addition of new fields in desired business objects. The wizard opens the Custom Field App with the business contexts pre set.

 

Custom Logic

Enables addition of custom logic via Cloud BAdIs. Here as well the wizard opens the Custom Logic App with the business contexts pre set.

 

Side-by-Side

Custom Process

Create a Process in SAP Build Process Automation that starts based on an event from SAP S/4HANA. The business context of the starting SAP S/4HANA app is pre set in the wizard to easily identify the relevant events.

A detailed explanation of this process extension option is available in this blog from my colleague.

The extensibility wizard is expected to grow with more extension options in future.

 

Conclusion

The new SAP Build Extensibility wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides a simple guided experience to create extensions both on-stack and side-by-side.

If you have access to an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system, follow the setup steps and do explore the Extensibility Wizard !

entry.title: Creating side-by-side process extensions from SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
The release of SAP Build's new extensibility wizard forSAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408 release marks a significant step in streamlining the creation of on-stack extensions (within SAP S/4HANA Cloud) and side-by-side extensions (in SAP BTP) to help keep the core clean. The wizard, which can be accessed directly from any business application that is enabled for key user adaptation, allows key users and even developers to easily create custom process extensions in SAP Build Process Automation (SAP BTP) that can seamlessly integrate with SAP S/4HANA Cloud. In this blog, we will explore the use cases for process extensions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud and how the new wizard is a game-changer for customers looking to enhance their business processes

Side-by-side extensions

Side-by-side extensions run outside of SAP S/4HANA Cloud (or other business application) but interact with the business applications using standard extension points like APIs, events, etc. Side-by-side extensions can technically run on any application platform, nevertheless SAP provides and recommends its dedicated platform for extension: SAP BTP.

What are process extensions

Many applications in SAP S/4HANA use SAP Business Workflow enabling preconfigured workflow scenarios to be reused in various situations. The scenarios can either be implemented without any changes or configured for your business processes by making minor adjustments. On top of that, Flexible workflows allow you to define condition-based workflows for the pre-configured approval processes. For example, the flexible workflow for purchase requisitions allows you to define one-step or multi-step approval processes for purchase requisitions according to your requirements. These are pre-delivered as part of the SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud. However, your use case may go beyond a typical pre-delivered approval workflow which needs a side-by-side process extension. Here are some use case patterns which warrants a process extension in SAP BTP:

  • Processes that span across systems.
  • Processes that reach the users who are outside SAP S/4HANA.
  • Use certain capabilities from SAP BTP, like SAP AI Services.
  • Any other requirements that are not covered in the standard processes and workflows.

Example use case

Consider a specific use case where you want to make sure that every invoice created in the system has a reference to a purchase order without user intervention. There are multiple ways this can be achieved including a process extension. A side-by-side process extension in SAP Build Process Automation (in SAP BTP) can check if a purchase order is assigned for every new supplier invoice created in the system. If not, then the process shall identify the right purchase order using SAP AI Services in SAP BTP like Document Information Extraction, Data Attribute Recommendation, Invoice Object Recommendation, SAP AI Core, etc. When the machine learning model could not find the right match, then it could inform the procurement department member for further action. This process extension would run alongside the standard procurement process, adding the necessary functionality without altering the original process.

Why SAP Build Process Automation?

SAP Build Process Automation in SAP BTP with its rich development tools, integration capability and pre-built content is the recommended process extension platform for SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud. SAP Build Process Automation has 500+ pre-built content packages for extending SAP and non-SAP applications. Most of those content is free-to-use and can be accessed from SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

With the SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition 2408 release, the extensibility wizard streamlines the creation of process extensions in SAP Build Process Automation. The process created in SAP Build Process Automation is deployed & executable in SAP BTP and can be triggered automatically when a business event is observed in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. 

Creating a process extension

Let us see how easy it is to create a process extension with the new wizard.

Pre-requisite:

  • Setup SAP Event Mesh or SAP Advanced Event Mesh

A business event-based triggering of process is a capability of SAP Build Process Automation. It requires SAP Event Mesh or SAP Advanced Event Mesh to observe the events in SAP S/4HANA Cloud to initiate a process in SAP Build Process Automation. Refer to this blog for the detailed setup instruction.

  • You have the right business role and can access the extensibility wizard as described in previous blog of this series.

Procedure

  • From any application which is enabled for key user adaptation, open the 'Me Area' to choose the new 'Create Extension' option to launch the extensibility wizard.

Choose Process Extension. (If you do not have entitlement to SAP Build Process Automation in the connected SAP BTP tenant, then this option will be disabled with a 'Not subscribed' ribbon in the tile).

Based on the application from where the extension was triggered, the wizard automatically identifies the available extension points (in 2408 release of SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition, only business events are supported. More extension points is planned as future increments).

Choose Business Events, and proceed to next step.

The wizard also recognises the event objects that are relevant for the application and automatically applies a filter on the business object. Of course, you can override these filters and search any event that is needed for your use case. Select the appropriate event object and proceed to the next step.

All events of the selected event object are shown. Select the appropriate event and proceed to the next step.

Give a name and description for your extension project that will be created in SAP Build (SAP BTP). 

Confirm the changes in the Summary step or go back to edit any steps. This creates an extension project for based in the inputs given in the previous steps.

Once the project is created successfully, you can directly navigate to the project in SAP Build. 

When navigated into SAP Build (in SAP BTP), you can see a process automation project with a process created for you. 

When you open the process, you can see that the event selected in the wizard is automatically added. All the context from the selected business event is now available at your disposal. 

You can proceed to use the low-code process builder to create an extension process as needed. You can call any APIs like the SAP S/4HANA Cloud or SAP Cloud Integration models or any SAP BTP services, you can add a user task that creates a task in My Inbox or SAP Task Center, you can call an RPA bot automation, you can send notification emails, etc. Once the process is created, release a version and deploy the content into SAP BTP. 

When this process automation project is deployed in SAP BTP, it listens to the specific event in the connected SAP S/4HANA Cloud. When such an event is observed, it starts an instance of the process with the context that is available in the business event.

For more information on the lifecycle, refer to the help documentation of SAP Build Process Automation.

Key take aways

  • A brand-new wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is available with 2408 release to create on-stack and side-by-side extensions.
  • The wizard can be called from any application in SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition that is enabled for key user adaptation.
  • Discover the available process extension points based on the application that was used to start the wizard, through the concept of Business Context.
  • Create a side-by-side process extension in SAP Build Process Automation (SAP BTP) directly from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Look-ahead plans

  • Management of side-by-side extensions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
  • More extension points for process extensions apart from the business event.
  • Enable the wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition.

entry.title: Performant table list in SAP Build Apps
entry.content:
Managing and displaying large amounts of data in an application tends to require a lot of consideration, as it is easy to make performance suffer. I recently built a very performant list page for a S/4 HANA Cloud endpoint with 65k+ records using SAP Build Apps which will become available in a template later on, but meanwhile I wanted to share with you my solution so you can already adopt it in your SAP Build Apps application, if you are having performance issues in your list view.

The list page I created has a table type list with sorting, filtering and paging.

Preview of the performance of the table list I created

The main secrets of its great performance are:

  1. When page mounts, it begins to load 50 first items from the database to have something to display while it loads all of the items in the background.
  2. Even when all data is loaded, only 50 items are shown by default, unless the user goes to show the next 50 items, in which case 100 items are shown, and so forth. The way this is done is important – there is a separate data variable that only contains the items shown (BusinessPartnersFiltered). The sorting and filtering is also done into this data variable from the data variable that contains all the data (A_BusinessPartners1).

The core of this logic is placed on the page's logic canvas. The main logic for loading the data happens when the page is mounted, but there are events for when the user wants to see more data (but all data has not loaded yet) and when the data on the page needs sorting/filtering.

Logic of the list page

The page variables and their purposes are the following:

  • dataLoadFail and dataLoadSuccess if the fetching of the full data has failed or succeeded, some logic is run differently, e.g. the "Show more" button will not trigger fetching the next 50 items if all data has already been loaded.
  • showSpinner and showFilterSpinner show spinners in the UI, and block some logic from running by disabling buttons etc.
  • columns list contains the columns, their titles, order and the name of the fields. I use this page variable to avoid needing to change multiple places if I want to change which columns are displayed, or if I would want to change columns dynamically.
  • pageIndex, pageSize, searchQuery, sortBy and sortDirection are used for the implementation of paging, filtering and sorting by column.

As for the logic with which the items from the list containing all the items into the filtered list that is used in the list, I concocted the following formula:

 

 

SLICE(ORDER(IF(!IS_EMPTY(pageVars.searchQuery), SELECT<partner>(data.A_BusinessPartner1, SOME<column>(pageVars.columns, CONTAINS(LOWERCASE(ENCODE_JSON(LOOKUP(partner, column.fieldName))), LOWERCASE(pageVars.searchQuery)))), data.A_BusinessPartner1), IF(!IS_EMPTY(pageVars.sortBy), ENCODE_JSON(LOOKUP(item, pageVars.sortBy)), index), pageVars.sortDirection), 0, pageVars.pageSize)

 

 

This formula first figures out if there is a filter used (= searchQuery is something other than empty), and if yes, it only selects the items from the master list that contain the search query in one of the fields used in the columns. If no, the full master list goes into the next stage. In the next stage it checks if there is a column to sort by (= sortBy is something other than empty), and sorts the filtered master list based on this field into the desired sortDirection. After that the page is cut into size, which is usually 50, unless the user has pressed the Show more button.

There are many additional small details that make me proud of this particular list page, but this concludes the main ones that contribute towards its great performance. If you have any further questions about creating table lists in SAP Build Apps, please comment below.

entry.title: Build Process Automation Notifications Part 1 of 3 - Default Notifications
entry.content:
In September 2024, SAP Build Process Automation released standard notifications integrated by default into all running processes.
 
Now, with a simple configuration in BTP, all running tasks automatically generate notifications to the task recipient, keeping them up-to-date on their assignments wherever they are. Build Process Automation notifications use the SAP-standard Alert Notification Service, which means they are automatically sent over email, in-app notification center and mobile. More channels will be added in the future without any extra configuration work needed from administrators.
 
Here is how to get notifications:
  • enable the notifications feature in your BTP cockpit by following the adminstrator documentation here: https://help.sap.com/docs/build-process-automation/sap-build-process-automation/enable-notifications 
  • add a task to a process
  • run the process

As you can see, there is no need to specify notification parameters as the default settings already handle the most common cases. In this scenario:
  • the notification will be sent by email and to the bell icon
    the bell icon is the notification center in SAP applications, like SAP Work Zone
  • there is no need to specify recipients twice
    the recipients will be the recipients of the task
  • each user decides if they want mobile notifications
    the recipients of the notification can personalize, to turn off channels like email or notification center, or to turn on notifications for mobile
SAP Build Process Automation processes control finely-tuned sequences of tasks, decisions and applications in order to ensure that company workflows run predictably and error-free. When employees are required to contribute, notifications help get their attention precisely at the moment it is needed.
 
The notifications provide context and quickly let the recipient understand and decide what needs to get done. Opening the tasks in MyInbox or the modern SAP Task Center gives more details and actions. In a soon-to-be-released upgrade, links will point directly to the tasks and the personalization views.

entry.title: Integrating a Makerspace inventory to create an Immersive Experience using Build Code
entry.content:
This year we are celebrating 10 years of d-shop in Newtown Square. d-shop is SAPs internal maker space, a community collaboration that allows employees and visitors alike to experience new technology, share hobbies and turn ideas into reality.

There are ~39 community run d-shops globally withinSAP all with their own local flavor.

We have been fortunate in Newtown Square to have been able to support various conference and user group build projects. This has the wonderful side effect of leaving around a number of physical assets that we can make available to the local community.

We have Robots, Drones, Microcontrollers, xR headsets amongst our inventory. As a community room, we want the equipment we own to be used, not hidden away in cabinets. To enable this we have been using an Inventory Application that visitors can use to find and reserve items. Our original application was d-shop community built and ran on XSC. A maker space is there to enable innovation, so not only did we use it to track inventory and allow checkin/checkout, we also integrated with the physical room. When an item was checked in and out, Hue lighting would react, Amazon Echo and Google Home were added to provide voice interactivity. We tried NFC, QR codes and mobility to enable simple asset tracking.

 

Time has come to for a renewal, we are starting to see increased engagement in the d-shop, 3d printers are running at capacity, workshop requests are increasing. What a better time to take stock of the room and create a new integrated Inventory Application.

This is a journey, we are open to new ideas and integrations as we grow. Some ideas we have don't have any practical application beyond it being fun to try, others provide lots of benefit in both learning and practical applications.
As an example, in the past we created a d-shop coin virtual currency on Hyperledger, the idea was to reward returning equipment with credits. It was a fun group effort building and learning.

This time our plan is to leverage BTP and Build Code to help us rapidly create the new baseline for the inventory application. We are opening this project within the NsQ office to volunteers so others can learn as we build. We plan to share our steps in the community along the journey with our successes, failures, examples and perhaps seeding new ideas from feedback we get in the comments.

First steps, we will work on our MVP requirements and from that create prompts to help generate the first version of the application.

Watch this space....

entry.title: Unlock the Power of SAP Build and More During Devtoberfest Week 1
entry.content:
Devtoberfest is just around the corner, and we’ve got an exciting lineup of sessions specifically designed for you. Whether you’re getting started or looking to deepen your expertise, these sessions will provide hands-on insights into SAP Build and its latest capabilities.

 

Why Attend?

Attending these sessions will give you a front-row seat to the latest innovations, best practices, and success stories surrounding SAP Build. Plus, you’ll get the chance to interact with SAP experts and fellow Builders.

Join the event group to stay in the loop as Devtoberfest kicks off.

 

Devtoberfest 2024 Contest

By popular demand, we are bringing back the Devtoberfest contest! It’s a great way to stay motivated and engaged while you level up your skills. You can earn points from a variety of different activities to earn badges, swag, and the chance to win a grand prize.

Read more about the Devtoberfest contest here.

 

Mark Your Calendars

Don’t miss out! Be sure to check out the full schedule and reserve your spots for these must-attend sessions on Tuesday, September 24th.

  • Transform Data with Scripting in SAP Build Process Automation
  • Develop a Full-Stack CAP Application with SAP Build Code
  • Develop with SAP Build Apps Like a Pro
  • Real-World S/4HANA Invoice Approval Process with Process Automation

Stay tuned for more updates— We look forward to seeing you at Devtoberfest!

entry.title: SAP Developer News, September 19th, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast version: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/09/19/sap-developer-news-september-19th-2024/

DESCRIPTION

Devtoberfest Launch

  • Devtoberfest Community Home: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/gh-p/Devtoberfest
  • Devtoberfest Events Calendar: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/eb-p/devtoberfest-events?calendar=true
  • Devtoberfest Gameboard: https://devrel-tools-prod-scn-badges-srv.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/devtoberfestContest/scnId.Here
  • Devtoberfest Playlist on YouTube: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQDHz97qsNTNAE2jnUKj8X5d&si=R26dQIns-1e97fU0

SAP Cloud Application Programming Model August 2024 Release

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/aug24

Devtoberfest Crossword Puzzle

  • https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/devtoberfest2024-sap-crossword.html

Transporting content in SAP Build Apps

  •  https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-builders-blog-posts/transporting-content-in-sap-build-apps-sap-cloud-transport-management/ba-p/13737478

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro
0:10 Devtoberfest Launch
2:09 SAP Cloud Application Programming Model August 2024 Release
7:56 Devtoberfest Puzzle
9:06 Transporting content in SAP Build Apps

Transcript

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for September 19th, 2024.

[Sheena] Hello SAP community. The much-awaited Detoberfest 2024 is here, running from September 23rd to October 25th. Devtoberfest helps to prepare for SAP TechEd and offers a mix of educational sessions, hands-on developer tools, and fun activities like Petoberfest, Bugtoberfest, and Spooktoberfest. It is a point-based contest, and the Devtoberfest animated contest game board is already live. You can earn points by attending sessions, completing developer tutorials linked to weekly topics or engaging in activities that benefit the SAP Developer Community.

The grand prize is a retreat at an SAP TechEd Roadshow stop in 2025. Exciting, right? To start participating, join the Devtoberfest group after reviewing the legal terms and conditions posted there.

We will kickstart Devtoberfest on Monday, September 23rd, which is the ABAP/CAP Day. Week 1, we are bringing to you many exciting sessions in ABAP, mainly on clean core extensibility, building partner products on SAP BTP, major updates in embedded analytics, and data integration in ABAP Cloud with SQL services and CDS external entities. Week 1 also covers an exciting session on CAP, it's time to TypeScript (CAP edition).

On tooling Tuesdays, we'll focus on SAP Build Process Automation, Build Code and Build Apps.

Integration Wednesdays will have sessions on FlashPy, CPI Helper, and CPI Lint.

MAD Thursdays will focus on machine learning, AI, and data with sessions on SAP Generative AI Hub, SAP Datasphere, etc.

Frontend Fridays will have sessions on UI5 Freestyle and SAP Fiori tools.

Don't forget to participate in the fun Friday event Petoberfest by posting pictures with your pets at your workstation. So join us in this fun ride of learning and enjoy. Good morning.

[DJ] The latest release of CAP August of 2024, in fact, is out. And with it, there are improvements and fixes to the latest major release 8, which came out earlier this year, in the form of CAP Node.js 8.2.1 and CAP Java 3.2.0. Let me take this opportunity on my walk this morning to go through some of those highlights.

So first up, when the latest major release of CAP came out earlier this year, the new database services went GA, including the database service for SAP HANA. One of the benefits of this new database service for the Node.js runtime is that HANA native associations were no longer required to represent associations at the CDS level. This was great, mostly because it brought longer deploy times when we used SAP HANA Native Associations, plus there was the chance of indirect dependencies also being created. And if you're wondering about the Java runtime, the Java runtime hasn't used native SAP HANA Associations for a while. Anyway, at the time, the release notes advised us to switch off the HANA native associations using configuration. With this August 2024 release, that switch is now automatic. When you start a new project and do, for example, CDS add HANA, you get a switch in the configuration that turns these native associations off by default.

In the area of the CDS language and compiler, you can now use CDS deploy dash dash script to generate a SQL script as a starting point or manual schema migration. Note that in contrast to the existing dash dash dry option, it accepts and produces DDL statements that could lead to data loss. So are A, not supported for automatic schema migration and B, contains relevant warnings in the right places.

In the Node.js runtime, there are also some important changes. One of which is that linked definitions, the class that is used to represent internally structures of services and more, now no longer have the convenience methods such as for each map filter and so on that you would normally expect to have to process the linked definitions as arrays. Because basically linked definitions are not arrays, they're more than that. So these methods have been removed. In my opinion, this is great because it reminds us that we have to treat the data as it is. And so one thing to do is maybe convert the data to arrays using the rest parameter inside, for example, an array, literal array constructor.

Another improvement is that you can now programmatically, i.e. dynamically at runtime, control and activate the CDS debug output at runtime. This means you can turn on debug for cat modules as you go.

In the Java runtime, there's now beta level support for recursive hierarchies with Odata v4 in SAP HANA. Recursive hierarchy is an experimental term in the OData hierarchy vocabulary and is used, for example, with SAP Fiori tree tables. Treetables. You can also now define the name of the Java interfaces generated for CDS entities and structured types using a new annotation. In the multi-tenancy area, activating extensions using the SAP extensibility service can now be done asynchronously. You achieve this by sending a prefer header in the HTTP request with the value of respond async. This in my opinion is a really neat way of coordinating this using the RFC 7240 standard authored by an old acquaintance of mine.

Well I'm almost home but there's just time to tell you about improvements in the tools area. So first for all you IntelliJ users out there the SAP CDS language support plugin is now available on the JetBrains marketplace which means it's directly installable now. Great! For all you TypeScript users there's a new CLI command "CDS TSX", which in contrast to the existing CDS TS command uses a different faster engine. This feature is in beta still at the moment.

So I'm back home. There's one more thing to tell you about and that is if you want to use the SAP Cloud Logging Service instead of the SAP Application Logging Service, you can do that. And there's an option to use telemetry as well, including an option to use Dynatrace. What's not to like? Anyway, that's it for this August 2024 release. See See you next time.

[Shrini and Daniel] Hello, everybody. I'm at Tbilisi, Georgia with Daniel at the EPUB office doing SAP Build CodeJam. It's really great to be here. Yeah, it's been a lot of fun and because it's only a couple days before Devtoberfest, we wanted to tell you something about the contest. So the contest you can earn enough points to get a trip to TechEd perhaps next year and one of the ways to get points is by doing the traditional SAP crossword. So there are topics about SAP Build and JavaScript and even a reference to Analytics Cloud and it would help to know something about Sabrina Carpenter. Can anybody do the Cross Word? Yes, definitely. Of course, it helps to know a little bit about SAP. There are 33 questions. Fairly easy, a few tougher ones, but I think everybody can do it, and it might even be worth a couple thousand points. And it's already published, so even before the Devtoberfest starts, you can accumulate points. So that's pretty good. That's good to know. Yeah. Good luck to everybody.

[Rekha] Hello, everybody. We all know the importance of having development, test, and productive environments during the application development. SAP has recently introduced project versioning for SAP Build apps, web, and mobile projects. This allows you to transport the released project versions between development, testing, and productive subaccounts on SAP BTP. A transport setup of Cloud Transport Management Service, CTMS, Content Agent Service, CAS, and BTP destinations are required to start transporting SAP Build Apps projects between the three tiered dev test prod landscapes. There are four types of subaccounts or tenants involved in the scenario. A source subaccount, this is where the actual development takes place. A target, under this we have two subaccounts, testing, and production, and a central sub-account to administer the content transport between the source and target sub-accounts. In order to enable the transport, you will have to enable the Cloud Foundry, subscribe to SAP Build apps and content agent services, create an instance of content agent, and generate a service key on the source and target sub-accounts.

On the central sub-account, you need to subscribe to Cloud Transport Management service, create an instance of Cloud Transport Management, and generate a service key. The MBS role collection has to be enabled in BTP cockpit to authorize the users for performing the transport activities. SAP Burn Lobby configuration has to be done in the control tower in order to set the sub-account type to decide if it's a dev, test, or prod subaccount. Then BTP destinations has to be set from source to central subaccount, and then from central to target, that is the test and prod subaccounts. Finally, set up the transport landscape using the Cloud Transport Management user interface. For more details on this topic, do refer to this interesting blog post by SAP Build expert Mari Heary. Link is provided in the description box. Enjoy learning and thank you.

entry.title: The Case for SAP BTP: The People’s Verdict
entry.content:
After a careful review of all the evidence submitted in the form of product reviews, G2 has announced the people’s verdict on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) in their 2024 Fall reports, and the news is good; SAP BTP has once again been identified by customers as a leader in the Cloud Computing Platforms, Cloud Platform as a Service (PaaS), Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), and Database as a Service (DBaaS) categories.

This outcome of course is great news for SAP BTP and consistent with prior judgements, which have been determined using a robust assessment methodology designed by G2, indicating a strong level of customer satisfaction with this technology platform. But the question could now be asked, what are the compelling features and outcomes that customers experience when using SAP BTP, which makes the case for this technology platform so strong? The answer to this question is possibly best brought to light by an examination of some recent product reviews, in the customers’ own words.

“It makes everything work together so easily…”

SAP Business Technology Platform is very helpful because it makes everything work together so easily. This makes managing complicated systems a lot easier. We can also connect different third-party apps, and that's possible with automatic processes and smooth data flow…SAP BTP helps us understand things in real-time, so we can make quick choices based on the most up-to-date data. It can be changed and scaled up or down to fit the needs of our business and save us money at the same time. Review from mid-market customer, 19 Sept 2024

“SAP BTP excels at integrating diverse systems…”

SAP BTP excels at integrating diverse systems, both SAP and non-SAP, across on-premise and cloud environments. It provides tools like SAP Integration Suite that facilitate smooth, real-time data flow between different applications, which is critical for businesses with complex IT landscapes. Review from small business customer, 17 Sept 2024

“…it brings everything together in one place…”

What I like most about SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) is how it brings everything together in one place. It makes it easier for businesses to build new things or improve what they already have, without needing to switch between different systems. You can use simple tools to create apps, even if you’re not a tech expert, and that’s really helpful…In the end, it’s not just about technology—it’s about solving real business problems and helping companies grow, and that’s what makes it so useful. Review from mid-market customer, 16 Sept 2024

“…flawless integration proficiency and dynamic automation tools”

The thing I like the best about SAP BTP is its flawless integration proficiency and dynamic automation tools. The platform combines with both SAP and non-SAP systems so perfectly which makes it a great choice for my diverse tech environment. Its cloud native infrastructure is extremely useful for scaling and the security of this platform gives me opportunity to handle sensitive data. Also the customer support is very helpful for any issue/query. Review from enterprise customer, 7 Sept 2024

A continual focus on development and innovation

I can say with confidence, that this news is uplifting to all members of the SAP team involved in developing, innovating and supporting SAP BTP, who continually strive to make this platform and its associated cloud services the best they can possibly be for the customers who use them.

“Thank you” G2, for once more awarding SAP BTP with these wonderful Fall 2024 badges, as well as to our customers too for their kind words.

Until next time, thank you for reading and have a productive final three months of 2024, and if you get time why not take a few minutes to read some of our many other SAP BTP customer reviews.

entry.title: SAP MDK integration with SAP AI Core services: Part 6 – Work Order and Operation Recording
entry.content:
In this series of blogs, I will showcase how to integrate SAP AI Core services into SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) to develop mobile applications with AI capabilities.

  • Part 1: SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) integration with SAP AI Core services - Setup
  • Part 2: SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) integration with SAP AI Core services - Business Use Cases
  • Part 3: SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) integration with SAP AI Core services - Measurement Readings
  • Part 4: SAP MDK integration with SAP AI Core services - Anomaly Detection and Maintenance Guidelines
  • Part 5: SAP MDK integration with SAP AI Core services - Retrieval Augmented Generation
  • Part 6: SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) integration with SAP AI Core services - Work Order and Operation Recording (Current Blog)

In the previous blog, we showcased how to setup the SAP HANA Cloud vector engine and understand Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). In this blog, we will explore how to record orders and operations using voice and free text input.

SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) integration with SAP AI Core services - Work Order and Operation Recording

 First, we will introduce how RAG is used in our use case. RAG is used during AI calls to generate specific form cells on the operation details page. For example, a water pump may have different details compared to a laptop. RAG ensures that the AI generates the appropriate operation details. Currently, the knowledge base, which we introduced in a preview blog, is limited to a few equipment types (e.g., water pumps, electricity meters) with very limited content. In a real case, there will be more equipment types, each having a large volume of information. In that case, we will benefit from the fast similarity searching capability of the SAP HANA Vector Database. In our blog, we will showcase a very simple example with the equipment schema as the only useful knowledge.

We will use a JavaScript function to retrieve the equipment schema from an instance of the HANA Vector Database. This schema includes both the common schema and specific schema. Remember the common schema and specific schema of the water pump as part of our domain specific knowledge, that we populated into the SAP HANA Cloud vector database in the previous blog? Let’s refresh our memory below.

 

Common Schema
[
  {"Title": "taskTitle", "Description": "Operation Title", "Type": "Text"},
  {"Title": "shortDescription", "Description": "Operation short description", "Type": "Text"},
  {"Title": "description", "Description": "Detailed operation description", "Type": "Text"},
  {"Title": "priority", "Description": "priority (high, medium, low)", "Type": "Text"},
]

Specific Schema for SAPPump:
[
  {"Title": "yearOfInstallation", "Description": "Year of installation", "Type": "Text"},
  {"Title": "length", "Description": "Length of the water pump in meters", "Type": "Text"}
]

 

Now we will use the JavaScript function below to retrieve the water pump equipment schema.

 

async function getEquipmentSchema(context, equipmentName) {
  const functionName = 'getRagResponse';
  const serviceName = '/MDKDevApp/Services/CAPVectorEngine.service';
  const parameters = {
    "value": `In JSON, Get the Common schema and the specific schema of the equipment ${equipmentName}. The common schema cannot be empty. The title of each property must be in camel cases. In camel case, you start a name with a small letter. If the name has multiple words, the later words will start with a capital letter`
  };
  let oFunction = { Name: functionName, Parameters: parameters };
  try {
    const response = await context.callFunction(serviceName, oFunction);
    const responseObj = JSON.parse(response);
    return responseObj.completion.content;
  } catch (error) {
    console.log("Error:", error.message);
  }
  return Promise.resolve("");
}

 

The above code snippet uses the MDK Client API's `callFunction` method to call the OData function `getRagResponse` on the `CAPVectorEngine` OData service. It retrieves the common schema and the specific schema of the equipment in JSON format.

The returned result of equipment schema is as below

 

Equipment schema: {
    "commonSchema": {
      "taskTitle": "Text",
      "shortDescription": "Text",
      "description": "Text",
      "priority": "Text"
    },
    "SAPPump": {
      "yearOfInstallation": "Text",
      "length": "Text"
    }
  }

 

Once the equipment schema is fetched, we will use it to generate a form cell

 

// Generate form cells
    console.log("Preparing form cells...")
    spinnerID = context.showActivityIndicator('Preparing forms...');
    const formCellSchema = await generateFormCells(context, equipmentSchema);
    console.log("Form cells schema:", formCellSchema);
    console.log(`Form cells prepared!`);
    context.dismissActivityIndicator();

 

Here, we will call the `generateFormCells` function with the equipment schema fetched above as the input parameter.

 

async function generateFormCells(context, schema) {
  const method = "POST";
  const path = "/chat/completions?api-version=2024-02-01";
  const body = {
    messages: [
      {
        role: "user",
        content: `Generate form filling cells based on the schema provided as mentioned. ${schema}`
      }
    ],
    max_tokens: 256,
    temperature: 0,
    frequency_penalty: 0,
    presence_penalty: 0,
    functions: [{ name: "format_response", parameters: formCellSchema }],
    function_call: { name: "format_response" },
  };
  const headers = {
    "content-type": "application/json",
    "AI-Resource-Group": "default"
  };
  const service = "/MDKDevApp/Services/AzureOpenAI.service";
  return context.executeAction({
    "Name": "/MDKDevApp/Actions/SendRequest.action",
    "Properties": {
      "ShowActivityIndicator": false,
      "Target": {
        "Path": path,
        "RequestProperties": {
          "Method": method,
          "Headers": headers,
          "Body": JSON.stringify(body)
        },
        "Service": service
      }
    }
  }).then(response => {
    const resultsObj = JSON.parse(response.data.choices[0].message.function_call.arguments);
    return resultsObj;
  });
}

 

Again, we will send a POST request to the SAP AI Core service via the AzureOpenAI destination, using the same AI Core service deployment as in previous blogs for anomaly detection and meter reading. The difference is that this time we will not upload an image; instead, we will use the equipment schema we obtained in the last step as the content.

 

        content: `Generate form filling cells based on the schema provided as mentioned. ${schema}`

 

We also use an LLM function call to specify the structured output we want from the AI service. Please note that how to define an LLM function call may vary between different LLMs. This example is based on OpenAI GPT-4. For details, refer to OpenAI's documentation (https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/function-calling ). If you are using another LLM, consult the respective vendor's documentation to understand how to make function calls or, more generally, how to connect LLMs to external tools.

 

functions: [{ name: "format_response", parameters: formCellSchema }],
    function_call: { name: "format_response" },

 

For our example, we make a simple function call to format the response into structured data by passing the `formCellSchema` below as an input parameter.

 

const formCellSchema = {
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "commonSchema": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "Refers to each item in the common schema array",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "title": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Title of the property. Must be in camel case and match exactly as defined in schema"
          },
          "value": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Value of the property. Must be an empty string if it takes in a string/number. Must be false if it takes in a boolean. Otherwise, it must be an empty string"
          },
          "type": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "If the schema object is a value of string or number, it will be 'Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty'. If it is a boolean it will be 'Control.Type.FormCell.Switch'. Otherwise, it is a 'Control.Type.FormCell.SegmentedControl'"
          }
        },
        "required": ["title", "value", "type"]
      }
    },
    "specificSchema": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "Refers to each item in the specific schema array",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "title": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Title of the property. Must be in camel case and match exactly as defined in schema"
          },
          "value": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Value of the property. Must be an empty string if it takes in a string/number. Must be false if it takes in a boolean. Otherwise, it must be an empty string"
          },
          "type": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "If the schema object is a value of string or number, it will be 'Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty'. If it is a boolean it will be 'Control.Type.FormCell.Switch'. Otherwise, it is a 'Control.Type.FormCell.SegmentedControl'"
          }
        },
        "required": ["title", "value", "type"]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": ["commonSchema", "specificSchema"]
};

 

The formCellSchema object defines the structure of a schema used to generate form cells. It consists of two main parts: commonSchema and specificSchema. Both are arrays of objects, each describing properties of form cells. One is commonSchema, which corresponds to the commonSchema of EquipmentSchema. The other is specificSchema, which corresponds to SAPPump of EquipmentSchema. EquipmentSchema  is returned by getRagResponse from HANA vector database in previous step. With the above code, MDK metadata of two form cells will be generated by the AI core service. For our example, it will be something like below:

 

Form cells schema: {
    "specificSchema": [
      {
        "title": "yearOfInstallation",
        "value": "",
        "type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty"
      },
      {
        "title": "length",
        "value": "",
        "type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty"
      }
    ],
    "commonSchema": [
      {
        "title": "taskTitle",
        "value": "",
        "type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty"
      },
      {
        "title": "shortDescription",
        "value": "",
        "type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty"
      },
      {
        "title": "description",
        "value": "",
        "type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty"
      },
      {
        "title": "priority",
        "value": "",
        "type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SimpleProperty"
      }
    ]
  }

 

Now we will save above metadata to a variable to use later.

 

    const pageProxy = context.evaluateTargetPathForAPI("#Page:MDKGenAIPage");
    const cd = pageProxy.getClientData();
    if (!cd) { console.log("Empty"); return; }
    if (!cd.OperationsData) {
      cd.OperationsData = [];
}

const schema = [...formCellSchema.commonSchema, ...formCellSchema.specificSchema]
task.schema = JSON.stringify(schema);
cd.OperationsData = [...cd.OperationsData, ...tasks];

 

`PageProxy` is a developer-facing interface that provides access to a page. It is passed to rules to enable application-specific customizations. Here, we use the `PageProxy` of the current MDK page `MDKGenAIPage` to store the above metadata.

Next, we will use above metadata to create a MDK page. Instead of creating page with static page metadata, we will use below Javascript rules to dynamically create page with above metadata.

 

import ParseKey from "./ParseKey";

function getFormCellSchema(context) {
  try {
    const binding = context.binding;
    const schema = JSON.parse(binding.OperationData?.schema);
    const results = [];
    schema.forEach(formcell => {
      let value = formcell.value;
      for (const key in binding.OperationData) {
        if (binding.OperationData.hasOwnProperty(key) && formcell.title === key) {
          value = binding.OperationData[key];
        }
      }
      results.push({
        "_Type": formcell.type,
        "Caption": ParseKey(formcell.title),
        "Value": value,
        "_Name": formcell.title,
      })
    })
    return results;
  } catch (e) {
    console.log(e)
  }
}

export default function NavToOperationEdit(context) {
  try {
    const pageProxy = context.getPageProxy();
    const schema = getFormCellSchema(context);

    const pageMetataObj = {
      "Caption": "Operation Detail Edit",
      "ActionBar": {
        "Items": [
          {
            "Position": "right",
            "SystemItem": "sparkles",
            "Text": "Edit",
            "OnPress": "/MDKDevApp/Rules/ImageAnalysis/NavToRecordPage.js"
          },
          {
            "Position": "right",
            "Text": "Confirm",
            "OnPress": "/MDKDevApp/Rules/ImageAnalysis/SaveData.js"
          }
        ]
      },
      "Controls": [
        {
          "Sections": [
            {
              "_Type": "Section.Type.FormCell",
              "_Name": "FormCellSection",
              "Controls": schema
            }
          ],
          "_Name": "SectionedTable",
          "_Type": "Control.Type.SectionedTable"
        }
      ],
      "_Name": "OperationEdit",
      "_Type": "Page"
    }

    const pageMetadata = JSON.stringify(pageMetataObj);

    return pageProxy.executeAction({
      "Name": "/MDKDevApp/Actions/NavToPage.action",
      "Properties": {
        "PageToOpen": "/MDKDevApp/Pages/Empty.page",
        "PageMetadata": pageMetadata
      }
    });
  } catch (e) {
    console.log(e)
  }
}

 

First, the getFormCellSchema function extracts the form cell schema from the PageProxy. Next, we will define the page metadata object pageMetadataObj. You may notice that in the FormCellSection, the value of Controls is actually the form cell schema from the PageProxy. Lastly, the metadata object pageMetadataObj will be stringified and used to replace the content of an empty page. In this way, a page like the screenshot below will be created dynamically.

Now, we are going to fill out the operation detail form to create a new work order for equipment maintenance. Instead of manually entering the required information, such as the year of installation and the length of the water pipe, we implement hands-free input using voice input from a mobile device. First, we use the NativeScript speech-recognition plugin to convert voice input to text. Simply add nativescript-speech-recognition to MDKProject.json:

 

{
  "AppName": "MDK GenAI",
  "AppVersion": "1.0.0",
  "BundleID": "com.sap.mdk.genai",
  "Externals": ["nativescript-speech-recognition"],
  "NSPlugins": ["nativescript-speech-recognition"],
  "UrlScheme": "mdkclient"
}

Then, we can use this nativescript plugin in a Javascript rule as below
export default async function VoiceInput(context) {
  let userInput = "";

  if (context.nativescript.platformModule.isIOS || context.nativescript.platformModule.isAndroid) {
    // require the plugin
    let SpeechRecognition = require("nativescript-speech-recognition").SpeechRecognition;

    // instantiate the plugin
    let speechRecognition = new SpeechRecognition();
    let pageProxy;
    let shouldUpdateNote = false;
    try {
      pageProxy = context.evaluateTargetPathForAPI("#Page:SpeechAnalysisUseCasePage");
    } catch (err) {
      pageProxy = context.evaluateTargetPathForAPI("#Page:OperationDetail");
      shouldUpdateNote = true;
    }

    // save speech recognition object to stop listening 
    const cd = pageProxy.getClientData();
    cd.SpeechRecognitionObj = speechRecognition;

    try {
      const available = await speechRecognition.available();
      if (available) {
        cd.isListening = true;
        cd.recordingRestarted = true;
        context.getParent().redraw();
        const transcription = await speechRecognition.startListening(
          {
            // set to true to get results back continuously
            returnPartialResults: true,
            // this callback will be invoked repeatedly during recognition
            onResult: (transcription) => {
              UpdateProgress(context, transcription.text, shouldUpdateNote, cd);
              userInput = transcription.text; // assign the result 
              // console.log(`User finished?: ${transcription.finished}`);
              if (transcription.finished) {
                DismissProgress(context, shouldUpdateNote);
                return userInput;
              }
            },
            onError: (error) => {
            }
          }
        );

        const pageProxy = context.getPageProxy();
        const sectionProxy = pageProxy.evaluateTargetPathForAPI("#Section:FormCellSection");
        const controlProxy = sectionProxy.getControl('NoteFormCell');
        controlProxy.setEditable(false);
      }
    } catch (error) {
      console.log(`Error: ${error}`);
    }
    //for POC wait for 10 seconds to finish speech recognition
    await sleep(10000).then(() => {
      return Promise.resolve();
    });
    console.log('The voice input has finished!');
    // DismissProgress(clientAPI);
    return userInput;
  } else {
    // voice input not support on web, use default input for now
    return userInput;
  }
}

 

With the above code snippet, voice input will be converted into text and stored in SpeechRecognitionObj. Above code can be embedded into a MDK modal page as below

 

{
  "Caption": "Use Voice or Free Text",
  "ActionBar": {
    "Items": [
      {
        "Position": "left",
        "SystemItem": "Cancel",
        "OnPress": "/MDKDevApp/Actions/ClosePage.action"
      },
      {
        "Position":"right",
        "Caption": "Confirm",
        "OnPress":  "/MDKDevApp/Rules/ImageAnalysis/SubmitTextToEditForm.js"
      }
    ]
  },
  "Controls": [
    {
      "_Type": "Control.Type.SectionedTable",
      "_Name": "SectionedTable1",
      "Sections": [
        {
          "ObjectHeader": {
            "Subhead": "Guided questions: ",
            "Description": "/MDKDevApp/Rules/SpeechAnalysis/GetGuidedQuestions.js"
          },
          "Header": {
            "UseTopPadding": false
          },
          "_Type": "Section.Type.ObjectHeader"
        },
        {
          "_Type": "Section.Type.FormCell",
          "Header": {
            "UseTopPadding": false
          },
          "Footer": {
            "UseBottomPadding": false
          },
          "_Name": "FormCellSection",
          "Controls": [
            {
              "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.Note",
              "_Name": "NoteFormCell",
              "IsEditable": true,
              "Enabled": true,
              "MaxNumberOfLines": 0,  
              "PlaceHolder": "Your response to the question for form completion.",      
              "validationProperties": {
                "ValidationMessage": "Validation Message",
                "ValidationMessageColor": "ff0000",
                "SeparatorBackgroundColor": "000000",
                "SeparatorIsHidden": false,
                "ValidationViewBackgroundColor": "fffa00",
                "ValidationViewIsHidden": true,
                "MaxNumberOfLines": 2
              }
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "_Type": "Section.Type.ButtonTable",
          "Separators": {
            "ControlSeparator": false
          },
          "Header": {
            "UseTopPadding": false
          },
          "Footer": {
            "UseBottomPadding": false
          },
          "Layout": {
            "LayoutType": "Horizontal",
            "HorizontalAlignment": "Center"
          },
          "Buttons": [
            {
              "ButtonType": "Primary",
              "Title": "Record",
              "Image": "sap-icon://microphone",
              "OnPress": "/MDKDevApp/Rules/SpeechAnalysis/StartRecording.js",
              "Visible": "/MDKDevApp/Rules/ImageAnalysis/GetRecordControlVisibility.js",
              "_Name": "Record"
            },
            {
              "ButtonType": "Primary",
              "Semantic": "Negative",
              "Title": "Stop",
              "Image": "sap-icon://stop",
              "OnPress":  "/MDKDevApp/Rules/SpeechAnalysis/StopRecording.js",
              "Visible": "/MDKDevApp/Rules/ImageAnalysis/GetRecordControlVisibility.js",
              "_Name": "Stop"
            }
          ]
        }, {  
          "Header": {
            "UseTopPadding": false
          },
          "_Type": "Section.Type.Image",
          "_Name": "PlaceholderForPadding",
          "Image": "/MDKDevApp/Images/transparent.png",
          "Height": 10
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "_Name": "OperationRecord",
  "_Type": "Page"
}

 

The screenshot of this modal page is as below

You may have a question now: since the converted voice input is just plain text, such as "The water pump was installed in the year 2001, and the water pipe is 10 meters long," it is unstructured data. How should we identify the useful information from the plain text, input it into the form, and submit it to the OData service to create a work order data record? That’s a good question. We will again use the AI Core service to achieve this.

We will call following Javascript function

 

async function convertSpeechIntoStucturedData(context, userInput, schema) {
  const method = "POST";
  const path = "/chat/completions?api-version=2024-02-01";
  const body = {
    messages: [
      {
        role: "system",
        content: `Convert the following speech to structure data as defined in ${schema}. The properties in the schema
            are optional so if the speech does not contain the information on some property, do not include it.`
      },
      {
        role: "user",
        content: userInput
      }
    ],
    max_tokens: 512,
    temperature: 0,
    frequency_penalty: 0,
    presence_penalty: 0,
    functions: [{ name: "format_response", parameters: structuredDataSchema }],
    function_call: { name: "format_response" },
  };
  const headers = {
    "content-type": "application/json",
    "AI-Resource-Group": "default"
  };
  const service = "/MDKDevApp/Services/AzureOpenAI.service";
  console.log("Converting natural language to structured data...")
  return context.executeAction({
    "Name": "/MDKDevApp/Actions/SendRequest.action",
    "Properties": {
      "Target": {
        "Path": path,
        "RequestProperties": {
          "Method": method,
          "Headers": headers,
          "Body": JSON.stringify(body)
        },
        "Service": service
      }
    },
    "ActivityIndicatorText": "Converting natural language to structured data...",
  }).then(response => {
    const resultsObj = JSON.parse(response.data.choices[0].message.function_call.arguments);
    console.log("Structured data results:", resultsObj);
    console.log("");
    return resultsObj;
  });

 

By now, you should be very familiar with this kind of JavaScript function. We have repeatedly used it a few times for meter reading, anomaly detection, maintenance guideline generation, etc.

In the POST request body, the variable ${schema} is the same as the one we saved in the earlier step, the form cells schema. Similarly, as before, we also use an LLM function call format_response to specify the structured output we want from the AI core service. This format_response function has a parameter structuredDataSchema to specify the schema of the structured output we want.

 

const structuredDataSchema = {
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "formCell": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "Refers to each item in the schema array. The title property must completely match the one defined in schema",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "title": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Title of the property"
          },
          "value": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Value of the property"
          }
        },
        "required": ["title", "value"]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": ["formCell"]
};

 

Thus, the AI core service will process the input "The water pump was installed in the year 2001, and the water pipe is 10 meters long," extract the useful information, and adhere to the structuredDataSchema to output the structured data as shown below:

 

Structured data results: {
    "formCell": [
      {
        "title": "yearOfInstallation",
        "value": "2001"
      },
      {
        "title": "length",
        "value": "10 meter"
      }
    ]
  }

 

The output structured data will be populated into the form cells accordingly and submitted to the OData service to create a new work order for equipment operation and maintenance.

To summarize, in this blog, we provide technical details and examples on how to use the SAP AI core service in an MDK app to convert natural language input into structured data with the help of Retrieval Augmented Generation and LLM functions. The structured data is then populated into form cells and submitted to the OData service to create a work order for equipment operation and maintenance.

This marks the temporary end of our series on SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) integration with SAP AI Core service. Thank you for joining me throughout this series. However, this is not the end, but just the beginning of our MDK AI journey. We will continue to update you with new AI-related features for MDK, so stay tuned!

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Build – Q3 2024 Edition
entry.content:
Greetings, Builders! We’re pleased to bring you the Q3 Edition of our What’s New in SAP Build series. In this edition, we’re covering product-specific updates for:

  • SAP Build Apps
  • SAP Build Process Automation
  • SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Start, SAP Task Center
  • SAP Build Code

SAP Build Apps

Release management and project lifecycle management: It’s now possible to release and transport projects across different tenants using SAP Cloud Transport Management System. You can transport project versions between subaccounts while isolating development, testing, and productive deployment of SAP Build Apps applications to their own landscapes. To get started, see this blog post.

Performant table list with large dataset from S/4HANA: Managing and displaying large amounts of data in an application can be challenging and cause performance issues. Check out this example of creating a performant list page for an S/4HANA Cloud endpoint with 65k+ records - see the blog post.

Copying pages within one project: This feature enables users to copy and duplicate a page within a project in SAP Build Apps – a simple, yet powerful, add-on to further speed up development.

New Build Service: We recently launched a new and improved experience for deploying application packages. The build service includes managing image assets, iOS usage strings, mobile service enablement, package creation, and configuration management. The updated UI provides a much smoother experience for developers at the deployment phase, ensuring that every requirement is met and that your apps are ready to publish. Try it out today!

  • Also check out Ludo Noens’ series on Consuming SAP with SAP Build Apps – Creating a CRUD Application
  • Learn more about what’s coming up on the SAP Build Apps Roadmap.

SAP Build Process Automation

Script Task Editor: It's now possible to write JavaScript code in the new Script Editor, allowing the execution of complex data type transformations and additional operations on the process context. Previously this was limited to automation - now it includes process inputs and custom variables, facilitating advanced data manipulation and transformation. See more in the blog post.

Scheduled trigger: Now you can schedule process automations based on user-defined time intervals and dates at runtime. This allows you to enable system-centric and time-based process automation use cases, such as working and nonworking days in creating your automation scenarios to avoid resource mismatches.

Additional Outcomes in Forms and UI5 Support for Tasks: This enables citizen developers to configure additional outcome actions in the process to let approvers request rework in case of missing or wrong information in approval forms. You can also start filling out multiple forms efficiently through a guided experience.

Advanced Event Project Creation with Manual Specification Upload: This allows users to upload business event specifications for the creation of an event project in the lobby. The new feature adds support for business events across lines of business (LOBs) and custom events, including third-party events that are supported at runtime using SAP Event Mesh.

Process Visibility enhancements: These include actionable insights, instance tracking for requestors and additional monitoring capabilities via CALM. Now you can turn insights into meaningful outcomes and enable the triggering of escalation flows based on visibility insights, to directly react and resolve critical situations. This enables auditable and governed escalation resolution, instead of having disconnected communication through different channels.

More on SAP Build Process Automation:

  • See the What's New Viewer for more updates, and improvements.
  • See SAP Road Map Explorer for more upcoming features and focus areas.

SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Start, and SAP Task Center

SAP Build Work Zone, both editions

HTML5 Business Solution as a Content Provider: With the latest update in SAP Build Work Zone, you can now expose BTP-based applications as Work Zone content, like roles, spaces, and pages, in the 'Common Data Model' (CDM) format. As a result, you can easily configure and enable access to BTP applications from a Work Zone instance, even across subaccounts. See more in the Help Portal and this blog post.

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Define Sort Order of pages and spaces: Administrators can now configure how spaces and pages are sorted on the menu bar of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition. This feature is only applicable if you're working in the new experience view mode for spaces and pages. See more in the Help Portal.

Import and Export of Pages and Spaces: Content manager tools will be enhanced to enable administrators to export and import custom (locally created) spaces and pages between landscapes.

SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition

Integrated guided experience across SAP Build Work Zone and Process Automation*: Guided processes are now available as an artifact in SAP Build Process Automation. In addition to the previously available “no template” wizards, it is now possible to create guided, process-based wizards in SAP Build Work Zone.

*Note: This will become GA in October 2024. 

SAP Start

Directly navigate to product home pages of integrated cloud solutions: The SAP Start site offers a new product section to easily see and navigate to the product home pages of the integrated solutions. This enables transparency of available cloud solutions for the current user, and quick and easy navigation to launch the product home page of the desired solution.

SAP Task Center

ITCE partner page: SAP Task Center now allows connections with third-party task providers. The first solution to leverage this capability is TaskConnect, developed by ITCE in collaboration with SAP architects and technical consultants. The application will soon be available on the SAP Store as well, offering a comprehensive configuration guide, installation manuals and BPMN diagrams. For more information about TaskConnect, see here.

More on SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Start and SAP Task Center:

  • Check out the roadmaps for SAP Build Work Zone standard & advanced edition and SAP Start / SAP Task Center
  • What's New Viewers: Standard edition / Advanced edition / SAP Start / SAP Task Center 

SAP Build Code

New AI Features: In SAP Build Code, you can now use the Joule copilot, our generative AI assistant, to write code by providing natural language prompts. For a detailed explanation of Joule, please read this article. In the last months we added many new features to this capability including the following highlights:

  • Support SAP S/4HANA Cloud Service Integration for Data Model and App Logic: It is now possible to use Joule to generate code for your integrated SAP S/4HANA Cloud services.
  • Major UX Update of Joule in SAP Build Code: With the latest UX update of Joule within SAP Build Code we enhanced the usability, streamlined workflows, and created a more intuitive interface, boosting productivity and satisfaction.
  • Generative AI support for productivity tools: We now allow you to use Joule directly from the storyboard.
  • Use Joule to enhance your already existing data models and services: It is now possible to modify already existing data model and service entities using a prompt.

Why SAP Build Code: Check out this blog post by Product Manager Rupa Maity describing why to move to SAP Build Code.

Stay updated on the latest Build Code innovations: Do you want to stay informed about the latest product updates for SAP Build Code? Follow our tag on SAP Community and subscribe to our SAP Build Code Newsletter. 

  • See the latest update product updates from September 2024 for SAP Build Code.
  • Learn more on the SAP Build Code Community.

--- 

💡Please note this blog post provides a highlight of the latest features and enhancements. 

  • For comprehensive list of all updates, please refer to our Help Portal What’s New in SAP Build.
  • Join the SAP Builders group for product resources and community questions.
  • Devtoberfest is here! 🥨 Don't miss our SAP Build-themed sessions and more.
  • ...and that means SAP TechEd is right around the corner! Explore SAP Build at TechEd 2024.

Which feature are you most excited to test out? Let us know in the comments!

entry.title: SAP Developer News, September 26th, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast Version: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/09/26/sap-developer-news-september-26th-2024/

DESCRIPTION

Extensibility Wizard

  • Blog by Karthik Thirunavukkarasan, overview of Extensibility Wizard: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-build-extensibility-wizard-for-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/ba-p/13867427
  • Blog by Seshadri Sreenivas Ramanarayanan, on side-by-side extensions with SAP Build process Automation: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/creating-side-by-side-process-extensions-from-sap-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/13867657
